coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene under visible light using carbon quantum dot-decorated novel Z-scheme BiVO4/MOF-808/CN photocatalyst: Mechanism, performance and stability

coredata.dc:description:
Background Photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization technology enabling the facile oxidation of these compounds to sulfones, deep desulfurization, operation at ambient temperature and pressure, and minimal energy consumption.
Methods This study introduces a novel photocatalyst, BiVO4/MOF-808/CN integrated with carbon quantum dots (BMC-CQD), designed for the oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene (DBT) under visible light irradiation.
Significant findings Novel approach was undertaken by integrating the photocatalyst BiVO4, g-C3N4 with MOF-808 and carbon quantum dots to enhance the interaction between semiconductors. This innovative photocatalyst addresses several limitations associated with MOF-808, including enhanced visible light absorption (2.21–2.60 eV), reduced electron-hole recombination, rapid charge transfer, high surface area (1370 m2/g), large pore volume (0.908 cm3/g). Under optimized conditions of a catalyst dosage of 1.5 g/L, a reaction temperature of 50 °C, an O/S molar ratio of 6, and an initial DBT concentration of 500 mg/L, the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst achieved an impressive 99.5 % DBT removal efficiency in just 25 min. Incorporating CQD into the BMC framework significantly amplifies the removal rate of the DBT by 10.69, 2.13 and 8.7 times compared to the BiVO4, MOF-808 and CN, respectively. The radical trapping experiments have shown that the •OH and •O2 − radicals play a key role in the DBT removal process.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene under visible light using carbon quantum dot-decorated novel Z-scheme BiVO4/MOF-808/CN photocatalyst: Mechanism, performance and stability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization technology enabling the facile oxidation of these compounds to sulfones, deep desulfurization, operation at ambient temperature and pressure, and minimal energy consumption.

This study introduces a novel photocatalyst, BiVO4/MOF-808/CN integrated with carbon quantum dots (BMC-CQD), designed for the oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene (DBT) under visible light irradiation.

Novel approach was undertaken by integrating the photocatalyst BiVO4, g-C3N4 with MOF-808 and carbon quantum dots to enhance the interaction between semiconductors. This innovative photocatalyst addresses several limitations associated with MOF-808, including enhanced visible light absorption (2.21–2.60 eV), reduced electron-hole recombination, rapid charge transfer, high surface area (1370 m2/g), large pore volume (0.908 cm3/g). Under optimized conditions of a catalyst dosage of 1.5 g/L, a reaction temperature of 50 °C, an O/S molar ratio of 6, and an initial DBT concentration of 500 mg/L, the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst achieved an impressive 99.5 % DBT removal efficiency in just 25 min. Incorporating CQD into the BMC framework significantly amplifies the removal rate of the DBT by 10.69, 2.13 and 8.7 times compared to the BiVO4, MOF-808 and CN, respectively. The radical trapping experiments have shown that the •OH and •O2 − radicals play a key role in the DBT removal process.

ce:section.ce:para: In developing nations, the escalating levels of sulfur oxides (SOx) have sparked grave concerns over air quality within major urban centers [1]. Predominantly stemming from the combustion of sulfur-rich compounds in transportation fuels like gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel, these emissions contribute substantially to environmental degradation, notably precipitating acid rain and posing threats to both human health and ecosystems [2]. Consequently, there is a pressing need for the implementation of deep desulfurization processes in fuel production to facilitate cleaner energy generation [3]. A myriad of technologies currently exist to address sulfur compound removal from fuel, including hydrodesulfurization (HDS), oxidation-reduction, catalytic cracking, biological methods, adsorption, and extraction [4]. However, compounds like benzothiophene (BT), dibenzothiophene (DBT) and 4,6-dimethyl dibenzothiophene present significant challenges for conventional removal methods such as HDS, adsorption, or extraction. Despite the utilization of HDS operating at high temperatures (300–400 °C) and pressures (20–40 atm) alongside substantial energy and hydrogen (H2) to mitigate sulfur-containing compounds, its effectiveness remains limited [1].

Oxidative desulfurization (ODS) is a process oxidizing sulfur compounds in the distillation of petroleum into polar sulfones or sulfoxides through the oxygenation reaction of oxidizing agents (hydro peroxide, tert-butyl hydroperoxide) by catalysts [5]. ODS technology, operating within the temperature range of 60–80 °C, has shown promise in eliminating sulfur-containing compounds. However, the oxidation process at these temperatures compromises the desired fuel components, consequently reducing the overall octane rating due to the consumption of oxidizing agents within the fuel [6]. Photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization (PODS) is a technology that uses a photo-catalyst and visible light as an energy source to oxidize sulfur compounds into highly polar sulfoxides or sulfones and can be easily separated by extraction solvents [7]. PODS technology offers a viable alternative, enabling the facile oxidation of these compounds to sulfones under mild conditions. These sulfones can then be readily separated through adsorption or simple extraction processes [8–10]. PODS offers advantages such as deep desulfurization, operation at ambient temperature and pressure, and minimal energy consumption [11–13], further enhanced through the utilization of various photocatalysts, resulting in heightened efficiency and reduced reaction times.

Recent advancements have spotlighted bismuth (III) vanadate (BiVO4) as a promising photocatalyst for oxidative desulfurization under low temperature, ambient pressure, and visible light conditions [14]. However, the BiVO4 material presents challenges such as a low surface area (< 10 m2/g) and rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs (e–/h +), resulting in diminished catalytic activity and stability. Consequently, to bolster the efficiency of oxidative desulfurization processes, researchers have directed their efforts toward the development of heterogeneous catalysts employing a Z-scheme mechanism. The Z-scheme photocatalysts were a photocatalyst created by the combination of two or more semiconductors with different energy band structures [15]. The process of charge transfers between semiconductors along the Z-scheme pathway, meaning that electrons from the semiconductor with more positive conduction band energy will move to the semiconductor with lower valence band energy and recombine with the holes. The Z-scheme photocatalyst helps enhance light absorption capability, protect higher conduction bands, and increase the efficiency of electron-hole separation [16].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4 or CN), synthesized from readily available and inexpensive chemical sources, offers high chemical stability, a layered structure, and visible light absorption capabilities, making it ideal for the fabrication of semiconductor heterojunction BiVO4 catalysts [17]. For example, Raeisi et al. [10] explored the BiVO4-Ni(II) photocatalyst in conjunction with g-C3N4, resulting in enhanced efficiency in DBT oxidation, achieving a remarkable 94 % efficiency under visible light illumination at low cost and without toxicity. Similarly, Miao et al. [18] synthesized a third-generation photocatalyst, BiVO4/C3N4@SiO2, which achieved a remarkable 99 % removal rate of DBT under ambient conditions and remained stable for four reaction cycles. This heightened efficiency is attributed to the electron-trapping ability and the heterojunction structure, which enhance charge transfer and separation processes while improving light absorption.

Moreover, the integration of BiVO4 with metal-organic framework (MOF) materials has shown promise in enhancing photocatalytic efficiency through both adsorption processes and synergistic semiconductor effects. Among various MOFs, MOF-808 stands out due to its three-dimensional structure, high water stability, and notably, its thermal stability exceeding 500 °C [19]. However, MOF-808 exhibits strong ultraviolet light absorption and possesses a high bandgap energy (Eg ∼ 3.9 eV), restricting its application as a photocatalytic material. Therefore, modifications with visible light-absorbing semiconductors such as g-C3N4, ZnIn2S4, and Bi2S3 are pursued. For instance, Wang et al. [20] enhanced the visible light absorption capability of MOF-808 by incorporating Bi2S3, resulting in Bi2S3/MOF-808 exhibiting 1.76 and 3.21 times higher degradation rates of tetracycline compared to Bi2S3 and MOF-808 alone under visible light. Song et al. [21] developed an S-scheme ZnIn2S4/MOF-808 photocatalyst, demonstrating a CO2 reduction efficiency reaching 8.21 μmol/g.h, surpassing that of MOF-808 (1.03 μmol/g.h) and ZnIn2S4 (0.84 μmol/g.h).

Recently, recent developments have shed light on the potential of carbon quantum dots (CQD) as a versatile nanomaterial in enhancing photocatalytic performance [22]. CQDs, synthesized from accessible and cost-effective chemical sources, boast numerous advantages such as low cost, high chemical stability, strong electronic conductivity, and small particle size [23]. Notably, CQDs can serve as bridges, reinforcing the binding of semiconductor materials, while their excellent electrical conductivity aids in effectively separating charged particles [24]. For example, Rahmani et al. [24] improved the visible light absorption capability of Zr-MOF-808 by incorporating it with nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CQD). This modification led to rapid rhodamine B (RhB) degradation, with a 6.93 and 29.67 times increase compared to MOF-808 and N, S-CQD alone, respectively.

In this study, we aimed to enhance the binding of the components within the BiVO4/MOF-808/CN photocatalyst by incorporating CQDs using the microwave hydrothermal method. The resulting BiVO4/MOF-808/CN/CQD (BMC-CQD) photocatalyst was employed for the degradation of DBT in a model fuel sample. Various factors affecting the efficiency of DBT degradation, such as catalyst dosage, initial DBT concentration, O/S molar ratio, and reaction temperature, were systematically investigated.

The BiVO4 material was synthesized by a hydrothermal method. Initially, 10 mmol of bismuth nitrate was dissolved in 100 mL of 2 M nitric acid solution, and 10 mmol of NH4VO3 was dissolved in 100 mL of 2 M NaOH solution. These solutions were gradually combined and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was transferred into a Teflon flask (200 mL) and subjected to a reaction at 180 °C for 8 h, yielding a yellow solution. The precipitated yellow solid was then separated via centrifugation and subjected to multiple washes with water to obtain the BiVO4 material.

The synthesis of MOF-808 commenced by adding 1.68 g of H3BTC (8 mmol) to 60 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), followed by the gradual addition of 60 mL of formic acid (HCOOH) to the mixture, which was stirred until a transparent solution (solution 1) was obtained. Separately, 7.734 g of ZrOCl2·8H2O (24 mmol) was dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and subjected to sonication for 30 min to ensure homogeneity (solution 2). Solution 2 was then added dropwise to solution 1 while stirring for 60 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon bottle and heated at 100 °C for 60 min under microwave irradiation. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered, washed with DMF and ethanol to remove any residual substances, and finally dried overnight in an oven at 80 °C to obtain the MOF-808 material.

The synthesis of the BiVO4/MOF-808/CQD composite involved the microwave-support hydrothermal method. Initially, 20 mL of CQD solution was dissolved in a mixture of ethanol and water (volume ratio of 1:4). Subsequently, 1 g of BiVO4/MOF-808/CN, composed of BiVO4 (0.1 g), MOF-808 (0.7 g), and CN (0.2 g) (Text 2, supplementary information), was added to the CQD solution (Text 3, supplementary information), followed by ultrasonication for 60 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a Teflon flask (200 mL) and subjected to hydrothermal treatment using microwaves at 100 °C for 30 min. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool naturally to 30 °C, and the solid was separated via centrifugation and washed with distilled water. Next, the solid was dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain the 10 %BiVO4/MOF-808/CN/CQD material (designated as 10 %BMC-CQD). Similar synthesis procedures were employed for the preparation of the 5 %BiVO4/MOF-808/CQD, 20 %BiVO4/MOF-808/CQD, and 10 %BiVO4/MOF-808, as illustrated in Fig. S1, with the compositions of the raw materials detailed in Table S1.

The model fuel sample was prepared by dissolving 500 mg of DBT into 1000 mL of n-octane solvent. Subsequently, 0.1 g of photocatalyst was added to 100 mL of DBT solution (500 mg/L) and stirred in darkness for 60 min to attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Following this, 0.5 mL of H2O2 was introduced into the mixture, and a 300 W lamp served as the light source to activate the photocatalyst. At specific time intervals of 5 min, 2 mL of the solution was extracted, and the catalyst was separated for concentration analysis at time t. The reaction temperature was maintained at 40 °C using a circulating water stream. The concentration of DBT in the n-octane solvent before and after the photocatalytic reaction was analyzed using UV–Vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of λ max = 325 nm [25]. The removal efficiency of DBT in model oil at time t was determined using Eq. (1).

where%H, C o, and C t represent the removal efficiency of DBT, the initial concentration of DBT, and the concentration of DBT at time t, respectively.

Different concentrations of DBT (300, 500, 600, and 800 mg/L), various doses of 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 g/L), predetermined quantities of hydrogen peroxide (0.3, 0.6, 0.9 and 1.2 mL), and reaction temperatures (20, 30, 40, and 50 °C) are used to investigate the influencing factors.

The structure, crystalline phase composition, space group, lattice parameters, and average particle size of BiVO4, CN, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD materials were determined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. Fig. 1 A presents the PXRD patterns of the BiVO4, CN, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD samples synthesized by the microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The XRD pattern of the BiVO4 sample shows peaks at 2θ values of 18.83° (011), 28.84° (121), 30.51° (040), 34.71° (200), 39.69° (211), 42.22° (015), 45.56° (132), and 46.91° (240), characteristic of the BiVO4 phase (JCPDS No. 14–0688) [26–28]. The diffraction peaks of MOF-808 at 2θ values of 4.35°, 8.33°, 8.7°, 9.99, and 10.88° correspond to the (111), (311) (222), (400), and (331) reflection planes (CCDC No. 1,002,672) [29,30]. Peaks at 27.4° and 13.2° are attributed to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively, characteristic of the g-C3N4 phase [26]. The average crystal sizes of the BiVO4 and MOF-808 samples are approximately 26 and 30 nm, respectively, as determined by the Debye-Scherrer equation (Table S2). The Miller index and Bragg's law were used to determine the lattice parameters of BiVO4 and MOF-808 samples. The synthesized MOF-808 sample exhibits a cubic structure with lattice parameters a = b = c = 35.40 Å and α = β = γ = 90° [31,32]. The BiVO4 sample possesses lattice parameters α = β = γ = 90° and a = b = 5.18, c = 11.78 Å [33]. The XRD pattern of the BMC-CQD samples shows characteristic peaks of both the BiVO4 and MOF-808 phases. Notably, the intensity of the BiVO4 characteristic peaks significantly increases with higher BiVO4 content in the BMC-CQD samples. It's noteworthy that the peaks at 2θ angle of BiVO4 exhibit slight shifts due to structural changes caused by the organic ligands in MOF-808, acting as anchor points to form strong bond structures between MOF-808 and BiVO4, leading to partial contraction of the BiVO4 lattice structure [15].

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups, chemical composition, and bond formation in the BiVO4, CN, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD samples (Fig. 1B). The FTIR spectrum of the MOF-808 sample exhibits stretching vibrations of the -OH group (3600–3200 cm−1), asymmetric stretching vibrations (1617 and 1572 cm−1), and symmetric stretching vibrations (1443 and 1385 cm−1) of the carboxyl group [34,35]. Vibrations in the range of 748–656 cm−1 are attributed to the Zr-O vibrations within the MOF-808 framework [34]. The FTIR spectrum of the GCN sample reveals aromatic C–N stretching vibrations (802, 1205, 1211, 1394, and 1458 cm−1) and C=N stretching vibrations (1560 and 1630 cm−1) [36]. In the FTIR spectrum of BiVO4, stretching vibrations of Bi–O, and symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of V–O groups are observed at wave numbers 578, 720, and 818 cm–1, respectively [37,38]. Additionally, the characteristic vibrations of the –COO– group exhibit a slight shift towards higher wave numbers by approximately 3–6 cm–1, attributed to the integration of CQD functional groups into the BMC structure [39]. These findings confirm the successful synthesis of the tertiary heterogeneous BMC-CQD photocatalyst, corroborating the XRD results.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a highly sensitive technique used to determine elemental composition, surface valence states, chemical bonding, bond energy, and chemical changes in elements. Fig. S2 displays the XPS spectra of the BiVO4, CN, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD samples, revealing peaks at 159 eV (Bi 4f), 183 eV (Zr 3d), 284 eV (C 1 s), 399 eV (N 1 s), 516 eV (V 2p), and 531 eV (O 1 s). In the BiVO4 sample, the high-resolution Bi 4f and V 2p XPS spectra exhibit peaks at binding energies of 159.16 eV (Bi 4f7/2) and 164.46 eV (Bi 4f5/2), assigned to Bi3+, and binding energies of 517.11 eV (V 2p3/2) and 524.49 eV (V 2p1/2), assigned to V5+ [40,41] Fig. 2 .

The binding energies at 530.96 eV and 532.60 eV in the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectra of the BiVO4 sample correspond to lattice oxygen and surface-adsorbed oxygen, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the Zr 3d in the MOF-808 sample exhibit two peaks at 185.24 and 182.88 eV, corresponding to Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2 of Zr4+ [42].

The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of the Zr-MOF-808 sample reveal four binding energy peaks at 284.73 eV (C–C/C=C), 286.11 eV (C–O), 288.74 eV (C=O), and 289.95 eV (O=C–O) [43]. In the MOF-808 sample, the O 1 s binding energy can be categorized into three main peaks centered at 532.86, 531.95, and 530.56 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen, oxygen vacancies and the C-O bond, respectively [44]. The C 1 s XPS spectra of the g-C3N4 sample exhibit three peaks at 284.83, 286.33, and 288.26 eV, attributed to the C–C, C–N, and N–C=N bonds, respectively [45].

The N 1 s XPS spectra of the g-C3N4 sample display three peaks at 398.72, 399.69, and 401.18 eV, corresponding to the sp2N C–N=C bond, the sp3N tertiary nitrogen atom, and the C2–NH amino group, respectively [46]. For the 10 %BMC-CQD sample, the binding energies are 159.30 eV (Bi 4f7/2), 164.59 eV (Bi 4f5/2), 517.15 eV (V 2p3/2), 524.77 eV (V 2p1/2), 182.71 eV (Zr 3d5/2), and 185.08 eV (Zr 3d3/2).

All characteristic peaks of BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN individually are observed in the 10 %BMC-CQD sample. However, the binding energies of Bi 4f, V 2p, N 1 s, and C in BiVO4 and CN exhibit a positive energy shift due to the decrease in electron density within the BiVO4 and CN phases. In contrast, the electron densities around the C 1 s and Zr 3d orbitals increase, indicating electron transfer from BiVO4 and CN to MOF-808 [15]. Based on the XPS results, the electron transfer pathway can be inferred from BiVO4 and CN to CQDs, with subsequent movement of electrons from CQD to MOF-808. This electron transfer underscores the close interaction between BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, and the carbon quantum dots, aligning with the FTIR results Fig. 3 .

The elemental composition of the surface of BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, and BMC-CQD samples was determined using the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) method. The EDS spectrum reveals characteristic peaks corresponding to the elements Bi, V, Zr, N, O and C in the BMC-CQD samples. As shown in Fig. S3, the intensity of the elements Bi and V significantly increases with the BiVO4 content ranging from 5 to 20 wt%. Table S3 indicates that the content of C, O, and Zr slightly decreases, while the content of Bi and V increases significantly as the BiVO4 content rises from 5 to 20 wt%. The weight percentage composition of the elements in the BMC-CQD samples, determined by the EDS method, closely aligns with the composition of the BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN phases used in the synthesis process. Furthermore, the EDS mapping image of the elements N, O, C, Zr, Bi, and V in 10 %BMC-CQD demonstrates an even distribution throughout the sample Fig. 3.

For the BMC-CQD samples, an inverse relationship is observed between surface area, pore volume, and the BiVO4 content. Specifically, the 5 %BMC-CQD, 10 %BMC-CQD, and 20 %BMC-CQD samples exhibit surface areas of 1142.92, 1083.86, and 754.97 m2/g, with pore volumes of 0.742, 0.689, and 0.515 cm3/g, respectively. This reduction is attributed to the inherently lower surface area and pore volume of the BiVO4 material. Conversely, while pore diameter and pore volume decrease, the size of the BMC-CQD materials experiences a slight increase, likely due to the integration with materials possessing larger pore diameters, as shown in Table 1.

The morphological characteristics of the CN, BiVO4, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD catalysts were determined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image of the BiVO4 sample reveals uniform, nano-sized particles (Fig. 5 A). The (121) plane of BiVO4 was determined to have a distance value of approximately 0.306 nm, as corroborated by Fig. S4 and consistent with findings by Wang et al. [48]. In contrast, the TEM image of the Zr-MOF-808 sample exhibited a crystalline structure with sharp edges and an average particle size ranging between 80 and 100 nm. This observed particle size discrepancy between XRD and TEM methods can be attributed to the fact that XRD measures individual nano-crystals, while TEM captures clusters of these nano-crystals [49]. The CN material presented a membranous structure with brighter layers compared to both BiVO4 and MOF-808. In the case of the BMC-CQD samples, TEM imgaes revealed nano-sized BiVO4 and MOF-808 particles, and grayish plates characteristic of CN [17]. These observations affirm the successful synthesis of crystalline phases of BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN facilitated by the incorporation of CQDs.

Optical and electrochemical properties include light absorption ability, electron/hole recombination rate, flat band potential (Efb), and charge transfer rate determined by ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS), photoluminescence (PL), Mott-schottky, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques, respectively (Fig. 6 ). The UV–Vis DRS spectrum of BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, 20 %BMC, and BMC-CQD samples were captured over a wavelength range of 200–1200 nm. As depicted in Fig. 6A, the UV–Vis DRS spectrum of the MOF-808 sample shows a strong absorption peak at 287 nm, corresponding to a bandgap width of 3.98 eV [50]. This suggests that MOF-808 does not possess photocatalytic activity in the visible light spectrum [51]. In contrast, both BiVO4 and CN samples exhibit strong absorption in the visible light region, characterized by bandgap energies of 2.26 and 2.80 eV, respectively. However, these materials also exhibited rapid electron-hole recombination, as evidenced by the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (Fig. 6C). Upon amalgamating the semiconductors BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN into a single composite (BMC-CQD), the light absorption edge extended to longer wavelengths compared to the MOF-808 sample. This is attributed to charge transfer between the semiconductors and the enhanced light absorption ability of CQD [52,53].

Utilizing the UV–Vis DRS data, the indirect bandgap values (Eg) of the BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, BMC, and BMC-CQD samples were determined using the Tauc equation (αhν = A(hν - Eg)2). Fig. 6B reveals bandgap energies of 2.26, 2.80, 3.98, 2.50, 2.33, 2.21, and 2.55 eV for the BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, 5 %BMC-CQD, 10 %BMC-CQD, 20 %BMC-CQD, and 10 %BMC samples, respectively. Consequently, the BMC-CQD material exhibits robust absorption in the visible light spectrum with bandgap energy ranging from 2.21 to 2.55 eV, which is conducive to photocatalytic reactions.

The Mott-Schottky plot was used to determine the flat band potentials of the BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN materials. Flat band potential Efb is the necessary potential to bring the semiconductor band edges back to their flat position from the bent position at the semiconductor/metal or semiconductor/electrolyte junction. As shown in Fig. S5, these materials are categorized as n-type semiconductors, evidenced by their positive slopes [24]. The flat band potentials for BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN were found to be –0.11, – 0.98, and –1.33 eV relative to Ag/AgCl. Utilizing the relationship between the flat band potential on the Ag/AgCl electrode and the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) energy, the NHE energy can be calculated using Eq. (2).

where Eo Ag/AgCl = 0.1976 eV at 25 °C and EAg/AgCl is the working potential using Ag/AgCl (pH of 7.4).

The flat band potentials (Efb) of BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN were determined using Eq. (2) to be +0.52, –0.35, and –0.70 eV versus NHE, respectively. Considering that the flat bandwidth (Efb) of the valence band (VB) for n-type semiconductor materials typically ranges from 0.1 to 0.2, we chose a value of 0.2 eV. Accordingly, the VB values of BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN materials are +0.32, –0.55, and –0.90 eV, respectively. Based on the relationship between the band gap energy and the valence band energy (Eg = EVB - ECB), the conduction band energy values and the CB positions for BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN are calculated to be 2.58, 3.43, and 1.90 eV, respectively [24].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the semiconductor materials BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, 10 %BMC, and BMC-CQD were examined to assess the recombination of electrons and holes (Fig. 6C). Upon excitation at a wavelength of 300 nm, the BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN samples emitted at wavelengths of 450, 420, and 460 nm, respectively [54]. Upon combining the semiconductor materials (BMC-CQD), a decrease in PL intensity compared to individual samples was observed. A weaker PL intensity signifies a longer lifetime for the holes generated by the photoelectrons. The decrease in PL intensity for the BMC-CQD samples is attributed to the movement of electrons through the interconnected CQD semiconductor materials, resulting in a prolonged electron lifetime.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots were employed to assess the electronic conductivity of BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, 10 %BMC, and BMC-CQD materials (Fig. 6D). In the EIS plot, the CN sample displayed a relatively wide semicircle diameter (3000 Ω), indicating poor charge transfer capability and consequently low efficiency in separating electrons and holes generated under visible light irradiation [16].

In contrast, BiVO4 and MOF-808 samples exhibit smaller semicircle diameters, suggesting a more efficient pathway for charge carrier transport. Upon integrating the semiconductor materials without CQD (10 %BMC), the semicircle diameter significantly increases, reflecting the poor charge transfer capability inherited from the parent CN. Conversely, the BMC samples integrated with CQD (BMC-CQD) show a significant reduction in semicircle diameter, indicating that CQD enhances the charge transfer properties of the material. This rapid charge transfer is advantageous for the electron-hole separation process, thereby boosting photocatalytic efficiency [24,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of BiVO4, CN, MOF-808 and BMC-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of BiVO4, CN, MOF-808, and BMC-CQD samples was investigated through the removal of DBT under visible light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 7 A, BiVO4 and CN exhibit low DBT removal efficiencies of 23.08 % and 29.14 %, respectively. This can be attributed to their low surface area and rapid electron-hole (e–/h +) recombination rates (Table 1 and Fig. 6C). In contrast, the MOF-808 material demonstrates a significantly higher DBT removal efficiency of 76.88 %. This elevated efficiency can be attributed to the numerous Lewis acid and Brønsted acid sites on its surface, facilitating easy reaction with the reactive agent H2O2 to form •OH radicals. Remarkably, the BMCCQD samples display superior DBT removal efficiency compared to individual BiVO4, MOF-808, and CN samples. This enhancement can be attributed to (i) the synergistic effect of active phases, (ii) increased defect sites, (iii) reduced recombination rate of photogenerated holes and electrons, (iv) enhanced visible light absorption, and (v) improved generation and movement of charge carriers due to the strong bonding between semiconductor materials facilitated by CQD.

The DBT removal efficiencies of the 5 %BMC-CQD, 10 %BMC-CQD, and 20 %BMC-CQD samples were found to be 91.75 %, 96.48 %, and 86.12 % respectively, after 30 min under visible light irradiation. A noticeable increase in DBT removal efficiency was observed as the BiVO4 content increased from 5 to 10 wt%. However, a subsequent decrease in efficiency was observed when the BiVO4 content was further increased from 10 to 20 wt%. This decline can be attributed to the substantial reduction in surface area of the 20 %BMC-CQD sample (Table 1), and the accelerated recombination rate of photogenerated holes and electrons compared to the 5 %BMC-CQD and 10 %BMC-CQD samples. For the 10 %BMC and 10 %BMC-CQD samples, the DBT removal efficiencies were 78.66 % and 91.75 % respectively. This indicates that the integration of CQD into the 10 %BMC sample increased the DBT removal rate by 1.78 times. This enhancement can be attributed to the improved visible light absorption by CQD and the enhanced binding between semiconductor materials.

In Fig. 7B, the reaction rate constants for the BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, 5 %BMC-CQD, 10 %BMC-CQD, and 20 %BMC-CQD photocatalysts are recorded as 0.0105, 0.0526, 0.0129, 0.0898, 0.1123, 0.0722, and 0.0638 min−1, respectively. Consequently, the DBT oxidation rate of the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst surpasses that of the BiVO4, MOF-808, CN, and 10 %BMC photocatalysts by 10.69, 2.13, 8.7, and 1.78 times, respectively.

To assess the influence of light on electron-hole separation, we conducted an ODS experiment using H2O2 as the oxidizing agent without illumination (Fig. 7C). Under these conditions, the MOF-808 catalyst achieved a 75.23 % DBT removal efficiency after 30 min, nearly equivalent to 76.88 % observed under illuminated conditions. This similarity suggests that MOF-808′s effectiveness in DBT removal is not enhanced by visible light due to its high bandgap energy, which hampers electron-hole separation.

In contrast, the BMC-CQD samples ODS process exhibited lower DBT removal efficiency compared to MOF-808. Specifically, the removal efficiencies for MOF-808, 5 %BMC-CQD, 10 %BMC-CQD, and 20 %BMC-CQD were 75.23 %, 70.42 %, 67.38 %, and 56.94 %, respectively. This decline can be attributed to the reduced Zr content in the BMC-CQD samples compared to MOF-808 (Table S2), leading to fewer acid sites and reduced ODS process efficiency. The 10 %BMC-CQD sample demonstrated ODS and PODS efficiencies of 67.38 % and 96.48 %, respectively, underscoring that the PODS process on the 10 %BMCCQD photocatalyst is more effective for DBT removal.
Several factors affect the DBT removal process, including photocatalyst dosage, initial DBT concentration, O/S ratio, and temperature. We examined these factors to understand their impact.

Fig. 7D illustrates the influence of 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst dosage on DBT oxidative desulfurization efficiency. The DBT removal efficiency increases with the increasing dose of 10 %BMC photocatalyst. Specifically, at dosages of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2 g/L, the DBT removal efficiencies are 85.56 %, 96.48 %, 99.05 %, and 88.75 %, respectively. As the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst dosage increases from 0.5 to 1.5 g/L, the DBT treatment efficiency rises due to the enhanced activity centers, facilitating increased substance interaction [56]. However, the DBT removal efficiency drops significantly from 99.05 % to 88.75 % when the dosage increases from 1.5 to 2.0 g/L. This decline is attributed to the formation of suspended colloids by the photocatalyst in the solvent, obstructing light-photocatalyst interaction [57]. With a 99 % DBT removal efficiency, a 1.5 g/L dosage of 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst demonstrates the highest efficiency (99 %) for DBT, making it the optimal dosage for subsequent investigations.

Fig. 7E shows that as the initial concentration of DBT decreases, the DBT removal efficiency increases. Specifically, at the concentrations of 300, 500, 600, 700, 1000, 1500 and 2000 mg/L, the DBT removal efficiencies are 99.12, 99.05, 96.48, 89.86, 81.03, 67.64 and 54.12 %, respectively. This indicates that higher initial DBT concentrations require more time for the 10 %BMC-CQD catalyst to convert DBT to DBT-O2.

The concentration of the oxidizing agent directly affects the rate and efficiency of DBT removal. Fig. 7F reveals that the O/S molar ratio follows the sequence of 6 > 4 > 2, resulting in DBT removal efficiencies of 99.05, 93.12, and 84.01 % respectively. However, the DBT removal efficiency declines as the O/S molar ratio increases from 6 to 8. This could be attributed to an excessive increase in the amount of H2O2, leading to an elevated proportion of H2O in H2O2. This, in turn, may hinder the surface diffusion process of oil/water or result in the occupation of Zr active sites in the catalyst [58,59].

Fig. 7G illustrates the significant influence of temperature on DBT removal efficiency, where an increase in temperature correlates with improved reaction efficiency. Specifically, as the reaction temperature rises from 20 to 50 °C, the DBT removal efficiency improves from 83.30 % to 99.52 % after a 25-minute reaction. This temperature elevation promotes a higher collision rate between reactants and enhances the solubility of the oxidizing agent in the oil phase, facilitating the chemical reaction between DBT and the oxidizing agent [56]. Consequently, elevated temperatures are advantageous for the DBT removal process. The relationship between 1/T (K) and lnk was plotted to determine the activation energy of the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst (Fig. S6), revealing an activation energy of 34.68 KJ/mol. Remarkably, this activation energy is notably lower than the reported values of 46, 55, and 65 KJ/mol found in the literature [60–62]. The 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst has been used to removal sulfur compounds in gasoline samples, under optimal reaction conditions: catalyst amount of 1.5 g/L, O/S ratio = 6/1, temperature of 50 °C, initial sulfur concentration of 2480.6 mg/L, and 300 W xenon lamp. Under these conditions, the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst removal sulfur from 2480.6 mg/L to below 9.5 mg/L, achieving a removal efficiency of 99.63 % in the real fuel sample after a reaction time of 60 min. This result demonstrates the effectiveness of 10 %BMC-CQD material in deep oxidation of sulfur compounds in fuel. Table 2 further demonstrates that the 10 %BiVO4/MOF-808/CN/CQD material showcases superior desulfurization performance compared to previously reported materials.

The significance of reaction radicals in the transformation of DBT to DBT-O2 was investigated through radical reaction experiments. To capture h +, •O2 −, and •OH radicals, we utilized ammonium oxalate monohydrate (AOM), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and isopropanol (IPA), respectively. Fig. 8 A shows that all three radicals, h +, •O2 −, and •OH, are crucial in the conversion of DBT to DBT-O2. Upon introducing radical-capturing substances IPA, p-BQ and AOM, the DBT removal efficiencies were found to be 73.95, 84.68 and 86.75 %, respectively. Thus, the roles of the radicals can be ranked as •OH > •O2 − > h +, with the •OH radical being most pivotal in the conversion of DBT to DBT-O2.

The primary products resulting from the DBT oxidation process were identified through GC-MS analysis (Fig. S7). This analysis revealed that the initial DBT sample (m/z 184.11) exhibited high intensity, which gradually diminished, being entirely replaced by the DBT-O2 peak (m/z 216.10) post-cleaning. This shift indicates an effective deep sulfur removal process [1].

Drawing from the electrochemical properties, the role of reaction radicals, and electronic transitions identified through XPS results, we delineate the band structure of the BMC-CQD material (Fig. 8B and C). Upon exposure to light energy surpassing the material's bandgap energy, electrons and holes undergo separation. Electrons transition from the valence band to the conduction band, generating positively charged holes and negatively charged electrons (Eq. (3)). Within the conduction band, electrons from the MOF-808 and CN samples possess sufficient energy to interact with the ambient oxygen, forming •O2 − radicals as depicted in the reaction (Eq. (4)). Moreover, these electrons can engage directly with H2O2, yielding •OH radicals (Eq. (5)).

In contrast, the conduction band energy of BiVO4 (+0.32 eV) is inferior to the reaction potential of •O2 −/O2 (−0.33 eV), preventing interaction with O2 to produce •O2 − radicals. Electrons from CN can migrate to the conduction band of MOF-808 (Eq. (6)), and the electrons of BiVO4 can transition to the valence band of MOF-808 via the CQD bridge (Eq. (7)). Within the valence bands of BiVO4 (2.58 eV) and MOF-808 (3.43 eV), holes engage in the reactions with OH− or H2O groups, resulting in •OH radicals (Eq. (8)). Furthermore, h + demonstrates the capability to oxidize DBT directly to DBT sulfone (Eq. (8)) [68]. Finally, the generated reaction radicals, including •OH, •O2 –, and h + facilitate the conversion of DBT into DBT-O2 products.

To assess the practical applicability of the photocatalyst system, recycling tests were conducted using the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst in the DBT removal process. Fig. 7 I shows that the DBT removal efficiency remains above 95 % even after five cycles of a 25-minute reaction, highlighting the catalyst's stability and reusability. This stability is further demonstrated by XRD, TEM, and XPS analysis. The XPS spectrum of the BMC-CQD sample post-five cycles shows no significant changes, affirming the stability of the photocatalyst during the oxidative desulfurization process.

Additionally, the bond energies of the elements Bi 4f, V 2p, Zr 3d, C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s in the 10 %BMC-CQD sample post-reaction exhibit a positive energy shift compared to the initial sample, attributed to the decrease in electron density during the reaction [11]. Figs. S9 and S10 confirm that the crystal structure and morphology of the 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst remain largely intact after five cycles. Collectively, these findings underscore the potential of the 10 %BMC-CQD heterocycle as a robust photocatalyst with excellent stability, paving the way for its industrial applications.

In this study, we have successfully developed a novel photocatalyst, the BiVO4/MOF-808/CN integrated with carbon quantum dots (BMC-CQD), which exhibits remarkable efficiency in the oxidative desulfurization of DBT under visible light irradiation. This innovative photocatalyst addresses several limitations associated with MOF-808, including enhanced visible light absorption (2.21–2.60 eV), reduced electron-hole recombination, rapid charge transfer, high surface area (1370 m2/g), large pore volume (0.908 cm3/g) and small particle size (80–100 nm).

The 10 %BMC-CQD photocatalyst demonstrated exceptional DBT removal efficiency, achieving 99.5 % removal after just 25 min of irradiation. This efficiency is attributed to optimized reaction conditions, including a catalyst dosage of 1.5 g/L, a reaction temperature of 50 °C, an O/S molar ratio of 6, and an initial DBT concentration of 500 mg/L. Incorporating CQD into the BMC framework significantly amplifies the removal rate of the DBT by 10.69, 2.13 and 8.7 times compared to the BiVO4, MOF-808 and CN, respectively. The radical trapping experiments have shown that the •OH and •O2 − radicals play a key role in the DBT removal process Kinetic studies further confirmed the suitability of the BMC-CQD photocatalyst for a first-order kinetic model, with an activation energy of 34.68 KJ/mol.

Furthermore, the BMC-CQD photocatalyst exhibited excellent stability and reusability over multiple cycles, demonstrating its potential for practical applications. This research paves the way for the development of efficient, stable, and recyclable photocatalysts for industrial desulfurization processes, contributing to the advancement of sustainable energy technologies.
